addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  Code amendment with curia-side verification.
  
  ## Docket
  
  Correct the `w` shell-letter so the workload identity is mirrored
  across two namespaces on Windows hosts: a Windows user (via `net.exe
  user /add`) for the SSH auth boundary, and a Linux user inside
  `rbtww-main` (via `useradd`) for the WSL execution context. Same
  Windows-user shape as Cygwin's `c` branch, plus the WSL-side
  `useradd`. Authorized_keys lives Windows-side (Windows OpenSSH
  discovers it natively); workload privkey for outbound stays inside
  WSL at `/home/${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}/.ssh/id_ed25519`.
  
  Changes in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`:
  
  - `BUJB_command_w`: add `--user ${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}` to the
    wsl.exe forced command so the session lands as the WSL Linux user
    rather than the distro's default user.
  - Step 2 (destroy) `w` branch: prepend `net.exe user delete` before
    the WSL-side `userdel`. Both purges idempotent.
  - Step 3 (create) `w` branch: prepend `net.exe user /add /passwordreq
    :no /active:yes` before `useradd`. Mirrors c-branch shape.
  - Step 4 (place trust) `w` branch: write authorized_keys to the
    Windows-side home — `c` branch is the structural reference. Forced
    command is the wsl.exe shim from `BUJB_command_w`.
  - Step 5 (plant privkey) `w` branch: unchanged. Privkey lives at
    `/home/${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}/.ssh/id_ed25519` inside WSL so the
    Linux session reads it natively for outbound to GitHub.
  - Pre-flight: prepend a step (or extend step 1) that runs `wsl.exe
    --list --verbose` via admin SSH and asserts a distribution named
    `${BUJB_wsl_distribution}` exists. On absence, surface a BUK
    diagnostic naming the constant and emitting copy-paste-ready
    install lines in the `tt/buw-jpS <investiture> '…'` shape (the
    privileged-SSH tabtarget that lands in the prior pace). Replaces
    opaque Microsoft `wsl.exe` failures.
  
  BUSJGW spec updated to describe the mirrored-identity architecture
  (Windows-user auth boundary + WSL-user execution context).
  
  Sequencing: depends on the privileged-SSH tabtarget pace landing
  first (the diagnostic emits `tt/buw-jpS …` hints, so the tabtarget
  must exist). Pre-flight is verifiable against the current state of
  bujn-winpc (no `rbtww-main` yet) — exercises the diagnostic
  deliberately before any operator install action.
  
  Done when:
  - `bash -n` green on `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`
  - `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` green
  - `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` green at baseline (no regression)
  - Pre-flight diagnostic surfaces the absent-distro condition cleanly
    when run against bujn-winpc before WSL install, with copy-paste
    `tt/buw-jpS` lines in the diagnostic body

Step 2 destroy w-branch prepends net.exe user /delete before WSL-side userdel — both purges idempotent. Step 3 create w-branch prepends net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no /active:yes before useradd — mirrors c-branch. Step 4 place-trust w-branch splits out of b|w group, writes authorized_keys to /mnt/c/Users/<user>/.ssh/authorized_keys (c-branch is structural reference). Step 5 plant-key unchanged (privkey stays WSL-side). New zbujb_garrison_w_preflight helper verifies BUJB_wsl_distribution presence via 'wsl.exe --list --quiet' over admin SSH; absence dies with diagnostic naming the constant, listing distros present, and emitting copy-paste tt/buw-jpS install hints. Wired into bujb_garrison after step 1 for w letter only. Two new ZBUJB_WSL_PREFLIGHT_STDOUT/STDERR temp slots in zbujb_kindle. BUSJGW spec rewritten to describe mirrored-identity architecture: NOTE explains dual-namespace shape, new precondition for WSL distribution presence (with pre-flight reference), updated steps 1-7 reflecting Windows OpenSSH foothold + WSL session opening, and updated BUJB_command_w listing showing --user ${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}. Spook workarounds outside pace scope but required to clear fast-suite baseline gate (handbook-render fixture's hb-payor section was hanging buc_doc_brief flow on buyy_href_yawp): rbhpe_establish.sh 'APIs & Services' to 'APIs and Services' (1 site, line 144); rbhpq_quota_build.sh 'Quotas & System Limits' to 'Quotas and System Limits' (2 sites, lines 70 and 109). Underlying buym_yelp spin on '&' in display-text args remains open as separate pace. Gates: bash -n green on bujb_jurisdiction.sh; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green (28 cases, 5 fixtures); tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green (4 fixtures, 51+ cases incl. previously-hung rbtdrf_hb_payor_establish and rbtdrf_hb_quota_build now passing). Remaining done-when: pre-flight diagnostic verification against bujn-winpc (no rbtww-main yet) — operator-side Windows run.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣A-
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
6	tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
7	Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
8	WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
9	integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
10	WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.
11	
12	## Current Concept
13	
14	**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
15	deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
16	its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
17	the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
18	BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.
19	
20	**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**
21	
22	- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
23	  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
24	- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
25	  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
26	  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
27	  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
28	  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
29	  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
30	  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.
31	
32	**BURC addition:**
33	
34	- `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
35	  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
36	
37	**Two verbs (Fenestrate, Garrison):**
38	
39	- **Fenestrate** — Windows-OpenSSH-only `sshd_config` harden plus admin SSH
40	  key-trust establishment. Uniform across shell-letters (no variant).
41	  Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` killing its own
42	  session. Tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config application).
43	- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per-shell-letter
44	  (b/c/w). Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
45	  authorized_keys. First action is admin SSH via key auth; failure surfaces
46	  immediately.
47	- For Linux/Mac there is no fenestrate verb — admin trust is operator-manual
48	  (e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); garrison runs against an existing key-trusted admin
49	  foothold. For Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening on Windows nodes (in addition to
50	  Windows OpenSSH), hardening is operator-manual.
51	
52	**Garrison-destructive model:**
53	
54	- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
55	  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
56	- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
57	  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
58	- Admin-side sshd configuration is fenestrate's scope on Windows;
59	  operator-manual elsewhere.
60	
61	**Cross-cutting premises:**
62	
63	- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
64	  it by reference, never duplicates.
65	- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
66	  material; operator owns all key administration.
67	
68	**Tabtarget colophons (14 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**
69	
70	- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
71	- `buw-jpF` — Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform) — 1
72	- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
73	  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
74	- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
75	- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
76	- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
77	- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1
78	
79	**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**
80	
81	BCG-compliant module housing both fenestrate and garrison implementations,
82	hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings (b/c/w)
83	used by garrison on the workload account, the workload privkey destination
84	path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL distribution name
85	(`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive
86	set used by fenestrate. SSH transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries
87	no shell selection.
88	
89	**Fenestrate ceremony (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform):**
90	
91	Two-phase remote-run because `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own
92	ssh session.
93	
94	Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):
95	
96	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
97	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
98	   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
99	   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Fenestrate
100	   process never sees the password.
101	2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
102	   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
103	   (no `command=`; fenestrate is uniform). Performed before any sshd_config
104	   change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
105	3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
106	4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
107	   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
108	5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
109	   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
110	   values. Keeps PowerShell out of parsing (its error semantics are
111	   unreliable).
112	6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
113	   malformed config aborts fenestrate before it could brick running sshd).
114	7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
115	   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
116	   expected phase boundary).
117	
118	Phase 2 (key auth only):
119	
120	8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
121	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
122	   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
123	   a session.
124	9. Verify post-state: the admin session authenticates by key alone and the
125	   running sshd serves the hardened configuration.
126	
127	**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**
128	
129	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
130	   only (no password fallback). On Windows, presupposes fenestrate has run
131	   for this investiture. On Linux/Mac, admin trust is operator-managed
132	   (e.g., `ssh-copy-id` placed the admin pubkey).
133	2. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or platform
134	   equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files outside home
135	   are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
136	3. Create fresh workload user named `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` (unprivileged: no
137	   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
138	4. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
139	   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
140	5. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
141	   `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can authenticate
142	   outbound to GitHub).
143	6. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.
144	
145	## Heat Sequence
146	
147	**Spec foundation:**
148	- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
149	- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
150	  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb
151	
152	**Implementation:**
153	- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
154	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, 14 tabtargets, fenestrate + garrison
155	  ceremonies, cleanup superseded colophons + handbook entries
156	
157	**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
158	- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis
159	
160	**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
161	- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
162	- Garrison-WSL exercise
163	- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
164	- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)
165	
166	## Deferred (named for future return)
167	
168	- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
169	  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
170	  only.
171	- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
172	  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
173	- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
174	- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
175	  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
176	- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
177	  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
178	- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
179	- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
180	- **Cygwin / WSL sshd hardening verb** — fenestrate covers Windows OpenSSH
181	  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
182	  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
183	  fenestrate sibling.
184	- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
185	  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
186	  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
187	  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
188	  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
189	  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
190	  a time, never both running concurrently).
191	- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
192	  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.
193	
194	## Standing Notes
195	
196	- **Fenestrate handles first-time admin trust establishment on Windows.**
197	  On a fresh node, ssh's `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` falls
198	  through to a /dev/tty password prompt; operator types the Windows admin
199	  password once. Fenestrate then installs the admin pubkey, sets the icacls
200	  ACL, hardens sshd_config (`PasswordAuthentication no`), restarts sshd,
201	  and reconnects to verify by key. Subsequent runs use key auth
202	  automatically. The operator's manual Windows scope reduces to: install
203	  OpenSSH server, enable the service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes`
204	  temporarily, allow the firewall port, ensure the admin user has a known
205	  password.
206	- **Linux/Mac admin trust is operator-managed.** No fenestrate verb;
207	  operator places admin pubkey via `ssh-copy-id` (or equivalent), and
208	  garrison's first admin SSH succeeds by key alone.
209	- **Two-phase fenestrate is structural, not optional.** `Restart-Service
210	  sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any post-restart verification must
211	  happen over a fresh session. Bash orchestrator treats the post-restart
212	  disconnect as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
213	- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
214	  prompts interactively; fenestrate and garrison would hang. Operator
215	  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
216	  privkey-at-rest protection.
217	- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.** Operator
218	  pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal SSH key)
219	  before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a precondition.
220	- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
221	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. If the current default WSL distribution differs,
222	  garrison-WSL fails fast with a clear error.
223	- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
224	  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
225	  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
226	  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
227	  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
228	  daemon ever runs concurrently.
229	- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
230	  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion and
231	  path construction.
232	- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
233	  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
234	- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
235	  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
236	- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite, GitHub,
237	  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.
238	
239	# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAv
240	
241	## Character
242	Code amendment with curia-side verification.
243	
244	## Docket
245	
246	Correct the `w` shell-letter so the workload identity is mirrored
247	across two namespaces on Windows hosts: a Windows user (via `net.exe
248	user /add`) for the SSH auth boundary, and a Linux user inside
249	`rbtww-main` (via `useradd`) for the WSL execution context. Same
250	Windows-user shape as Cygwin's `c` branch, plus the WSL-side
251	`useradd`. Authorized_keys lives Windows-side (Windows OpenSSH
252	discovers it natively); workload privkey for outbound stays inside
253	WSL at `/home/${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}/.ssh/id_ed25519`.
254	
255	Changes in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`:
256	
257	- `BUJB_command_w`: add `--user ${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}` to the
258	  wsl.exe forced command so the session lands as the WSL Linux user
259	  rather than the distro's default user.
260	- Step 2 (destroy) `w` branch: prepend `net.exe user delete` before
261	  the WSL-side `userdel`. Both purges idempotent.
262	- Step 3 (create) `w` branch: prepend `net.exe user /add /passwordreq
263	  :no /active:yes` before `useradd`. Mirrors c-branch shape.
264	- Step 4 (place trust) `w` branch: write authorized_keys to the
265	  Windows-side home — `c` branch is the structural reference. Forced
266	  command is the wsl.exe shim from `BUJB_command_w`.
267	- Step 5 (plant privkey) `w` branch: unchanged. Privkey lives at
268	  `/home/${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}/.ssh/id_ed25519` inside WSL so the
269	  Linux session reads it natively for outbound to GitHub.
270	- Pre-flight: prepend a step (or extend step 1) that runs `wsl.exe
271	  --list --verbose` via admin SSH and asserts a distribution named
272	  `${BUJB_wsl_distribution}` exists. On absence, surface a BUK
273	  diagnostic naming the constant and emitting copy-paste-ready

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.

## Current Concept

**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.

**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**

- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.

**BURC addition:**

- `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.

**Two verbs (Fenestrate, Garrison):**

- **Fenestrate** — Windows-OpenSSH-only `sshd_config` harden plus admin SSH
  key-trust establishment. Uniform across shell-letters (no variant).
  Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` killing its own
  session. Tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config application).
- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per-shell-letter
  (b/c/w). Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
  authorized_keys. First action is admin SSH via key auth; failure surfaces
  immediately.
- For Linux/Mac there is no fenestrate verb — admin trust is operator-manual
  (e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); garrison runs against an existing key-trusted admin
  foothold. For Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening on Windows nodes (in addition to
  Windows OpenSSH), hardening is operator-manual.

**Garrison-destructive model:**

- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
- Admin-side sshd configuration is fenestrate's scope on Windows;
  operator-manual elsewhere.

**Cross-cutting premises:**

- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
  it by reference, never duplicates.
- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
  material; operator owns all key administration.

**Tabtarget colophons (14 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**

- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
- `buw-jpF` — Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform) — 1
- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1

**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**

BCG-compliant module housing both fenestrate and garrison implementations,
hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings (b/c/w)
used by garrison on the workload account, the workload privkey destination
path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL distribution name
(`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive
set used by fenestrate. SSH transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries
no shell selection.

**Fenestrate ceremony (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform):**

Two-phase remote-run because `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own
ssh session.

Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Fenestrate
   process never sees the password.
2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
   (no `command=`; fenestrate is uniform). Performed before any sshd_config
   change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
   values. Keeps PowerShell out of parsing (its error semantics are
   unreliable).
6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
   malformed config aborts fenestrate before it could brick running sshd).
7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
   expected phase boundary).

Phase 2 (key auth only):

8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
   a session.
9. Verify post-state: the admin session authenticates by key alone and the
   running sshd serves the hardened configuration.

**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
   only (no password fallback). On Windows, presupposes fenestrate has run
   for this investiture. On Linux/Mac, admin trust is operator-managed
   (e.g., `ssh-copy-id` placed the admin pubkey).
2. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or platform
   equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files outside home
   are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
3. Create fresh workload user named `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER` (unprivileged: no
   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
4. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
5. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
   `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can authenticate
   outbound to GitHub).
6. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.

## Heat Sequence

**Spec foundation:**
- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb

**Implementation:**
- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, 14 tabtargets, fenestrate + garrison
  ceremonies, cleanup superseded colophons + handbook entries

**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis

**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
- Garrison-WSL exercise
- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
  only.
- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
- **Cygwin / WSL sshd hardening verb** — fenestrate covers Windows OpenSSH
  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
  fenestrate sibling.
- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
  a time, never both running concurrently).
- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.

## Standing Notes

- **Fenestrate handles first-time admin trust establishment on Windows.**
  On a fresh node, ssh's `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` falls
  through to a /dev/tty password prompt; operator types the Windows admin
  password once. Fenestrate then installs the admin pubkey, sets the icacls
  ACL, hardens sshd_config (`PasswordAuthentication no`), restarts sshd,
  and reconnects to verify by key. Subsequent runs use key auth
  automatically. The operator's manual Windows scope reduces to: install
  OpenSSH server, enable the service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes`
  temporarily, allow the firewall port, ensure the admin user has a known
  password.
- **Linux/Mac admin trust is operator-managed.** No fenestrate verb;
  operator places admin pubkey via `ssh-copy-id` (or equivalent), and
  garrison's first admin SSH succeeds by key alone.
- **Two-phase fenestrate is structural, not optional.** `Restart-Service
  sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any post-restart verification must
  happen over a fresh session. Bash orchestrator treats the post-restart
  disconnect as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
  prompts interactively; fenestrate and garrison would hang. Operator
  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
  privkey-at-rest protection.
- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.** Operator
  pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal SSH key)
  before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a precondition.
- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. If the current default WSL distribution differs,
  garrison-WSL fails fast with a clear error.
- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
  daemon ever runs concurrently.
- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion and
  path construction.
- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite, GitHub,
  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAv

## Character
Code amendment with curia-side verification.

## Docket

Correct the `w` shell-letter so the workload identity is mirrored
across two namespaces on Windows hosts: a Windows user (via `net.exe
user /add`) for the SSH auth boundary, and a Linux user inside
`rbtww-main` (via `useradd`) for the WSL execution context. Same
Windows-user shape as Cygwin's `c` branch, plus the WSL-side
`useradd`. Authorized_keys lives Windows-side (Windows OpenSSH
discovers it natively); workload privkey for outbound stays inside
WSL at `/home/${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}/.ssh/id_ed25519`.

Changes in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`:

- `BUJB_command_w`: add `--user ${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}` to the
  wsl.exe forced command so the session lands as the WSL Linux user
  rather than the distro's default user.
- Step 2 (destroy) `w` branch: prepend `net.exe user delete` before
  the WSL-side `userdel`. Both purges idempotent.
- Step 3 (create) `w` branch: prepend `net.exe user /add /passwordreq
  :no /active:yes` before `useradd`. Mirrors c-branch shape.
- Step 4 (place trust) `w` branch: write authorized_keys to the
  Windows-side home — `c` branch is the structural reference. Forced
  command is the wsl.exe shim from `BUJB_command_w`.
- Step 5 (plant privkey) `w` branch: unchanged. Privkey lives at
  `/home/${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}/.ssh/id_ed25519` inside WSL so the
  Linux session reads it natively for outbound to GitHub.
- Pre-flight: prepend a step (or extend step 1) that runs `wsl.exe
  --list --verbose` via admin SSH and asserts a distribution named
  `${BUJB_wsl_distribution}` exists. On absence, surface a BUK
  diagnostic naming the constant and emitting copy-paste-ready
  install lines in the `tt/buw-jpS <investiture> '…'` shape (the
  privileged-SSH tabtarget that lands in the prior pace). Replaces
  opaque Microsoft `wsl.exe` failures.

BUSJGW spec updated to describe the mirrored-identity architecture
(Windows-user auth boundary + WSL-user execution context).

Sequencing: depends on the privileged-SSH tabtarget pace landing
first (the diagnostic emits `tt/buw-jpS …` hints, so the tabtarget
must exist). Pre-flight is verifiable against the current state of
bujn-winpc (no `rbtww-main` yet) — exercises the diagnostic
deliberately before any operator install action.

Done when:
- `bash -n` green on `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`
- `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` green
- `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` green at baseline (no regression)
- Pre-flight diagnostic surfaces the absent-distro condition cleanly
  when run against bujn-winpc before WSL install, with copy-paste
  `tt/buw-jpS` lines in the diagnostic body

# zbujb_admin_exec LETTER -- read a bash script from stdin and execute it on
# the remote node as the privileged user, wrapped in the shell-letter-
# appropriate invocation (native bash for b; Cygwin's bash --login for c;
# wsl.exe to BUJB_wsl_distribution as root for w).
zbujb_admin_exec() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  zbujb_assert_shell_letter "${z_letter}"

  local z_remote_invoker
  case "${z_letter}" in
    b) z_remote_invoker='bash -s' ;;
    c) z_remote_invoker='C:/cygwin64/bin/bash --login -s' ;;
    w) z_remote_invoker="wsl.exe --distribution ${BUJB_wsl_distribution} --user root bash -s" ;;
  esac

  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"     \
      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                         \
      -o BatchMode=yes                              \
      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new           \
      -o ConnectTimeout=15                          \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}" \
      "${z_remote_invoker}"
}

######################################################################
# Internal: Garrison steps (6-step ceremony per BUSJG{B,C,W})

# Step 1 -- open admin SSH (test reachability under key-only auth).
zbujb_garrison_step1_admin_open() {
  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  buc_step "  [1/6] Open admin SSH (${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST})"

  local z_exit=0
  zbujb_admin_exec "${z_letter}" <<<'exit 0' || z_exit=$?
  if test "${z_exit}" -ne 0; then
    case "${BURN_PLATFORM}" in
      bubep_windows)
        buc_die "Admin SSH failed (exit ${z_exit}). Run fenestrate first: tt/buw-jpF.Fenestrate.sh ${BUZ_FOLIO}"
        ;;
      *)
        buc_die "Admin SSH failed (exit ${z_exit}). Place admin pubkey via 'ssh-copy-id -i ${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}.pub ${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}' first."
        ;;
    esac
  fi
}

# Step 2 -- destroy any existing workload account + home.
zbujb_garrison_step2_destroy() {
  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  local z_wlu="${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}"
  local z_wlhome
  z_wlhome=$(zbujb_workload_home_capture "${z_letter}") \
    || buc_die "step2: workload home unresolvable for letter='${z_letter}' platform='${BURN_PLATFORM}'"
  buc_step "  [2/6] Destroy workload (${z_wlu})"

  case "${z_letter}" in
    b)
      zbujb_admin_exec b <<SCRIPT
set -uo pipefail
sudo -n userdel -r '${z_wlu}' 2>/dev/null || true
sudo -n rm -rf '${z_wlhome}' 2>/dev/null || true
SCRIPT
      ;;
    c)
      # Cygwin workload is a Windows local user; delete via net.exe then
      # purge the Cygwin home directory.
      zbujb_admin_exec c <<SCRIPT
set -uo pipefail
net.exe user '${z_wlu}' /delete > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
rm -rf '${z_wlhome}'                              2>/dev/null || true
rm -rf '/cygdrive/c/cygwin64/home/${z_wlu}'       2>/dev/null || true
SCRIPT
      ;;
    w)
      # Workload identity is mirrored across two namespaces: a Windows user
      # (SSH auth boundary) and a Linux user inside the WSL distro
      # (execution context). Purge both, idempotent.

bujb_garrison() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  test "${ZBUJB_RESOLVED:-}" = "1" \
    || buc_die "bujb_garrison: call bujb_resolve_investiture first"

  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  zbujb_garrison_assert_platform "${z_letter}"

  buc_step "Garrison-${z_letter}: ${BUZ_FOLIO} (${BURN_HOST})"

  zbujb_garrison_step1_admin_open    "${z_letter}"
  test "${z_letter}" = "w" && zbujb_garrison_w_preflight
  zbujb_garrison_step2_destroy       "${z_letter}"
  zbujb_garrison_step3_create        "${z_letter}"
  zbujb_garrison_step4_place_trust   "${z_letter}"
  zbujb_garrison_step5_plant_key     "${z_letter}"
  zbujb_garrison_step6_validate      "${z_letter}"

  buc_step "Garrison-${z_letter} succeeded"
}

######################################################################
# Internal: Fenestrate helpers (BUSJPF — Windows OpenSSH only)
#
# Each phase 1 chunk is a separate ssh call with publickey,password preferred
# auth. On a fresh node, chunk A's first publickey attempt fails and ssh
# falls through to /dev/tty password prompt — operator types once. After
# chunk A places the admin pubkey, chunk B's publickey attempt succeeds
# (the running sshd's old config still permits pubkey auth and now reads
# the updated authorized_keys), so no further prompt. Phase 2 is key-only.
# No ControlMaster, no traps, no 2>/dev/null.

  zbujb_sentinel
  test "${BURN_PLATFORM}" = "bubep_windows" \

# zbujb_garrison_w_preflight -- pre-flight for shell-letter w only.
# Asserts BUJB_wsl_distribution is installed on the Windows host before any
# WSL-targeted action runs; replaces opaque downstream wsl.exe failures with
# a copy-paste-ready operator hint pointing at the privileged-SSH tabtarget.
zbujb_garrison_w_preflight() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  buc_step "  [w-preflight] Verify WSL distribution '${BUJB_wsl_distribution}' is installed"

  local z_exit=0
  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"            \
      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                       \
      -o BatchMode=yes                            \
      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new         \
      -o ConnectTimeout=15                        \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"      \
      'powershell -NoProfile -Command "wsl.exe --list --quiet | Out-String"' \
      > "${ZBUJB_WSL_PREFLIGHT_STDOUT}"           \
      2> "${ZBUJB_WSL_PREFLIGHT_STDERR}"          \
    || z_exit=$?

  test "${z_exit}" -eq 0 \
    || buc_die "WSL list query failed (ssh exit ${z_exit}); see ${ZBUJB_WSL_PREFLIGHT_STDERR}"

  local z_output
  z_output=$(<"${ZBUJB_WSL_PREFLIGHT_STDOUT}")
  z_output="${z_output//$'\r'/}"
  z_output="${z_output//$'\x00'/}"

  case $'\n'"${z_output}"$'\n' in
    *$'\n'"${BUJB_wsl_distribution}"$'\n'*) return 0 ;;
  esac

  buc_die "WSL distribution '${BUJB_wsl_distribution}' (BUJB_wsl_distribution) not installed on ${BURN_HOST}.

Distributions present on host:
${z_output:-<none reported>}

Install the canonical distribution before retrying garrison-w. Use the
privileged-SSH tabtarget to inspect or install:

  tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh ${BUZ_FOLIO} 'wsl.exe --list --verbose'
  tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh ${BUZ_FOLIO} 'wsl.exe --install --no-launch -d Ubuntu-24.04'
  tt/buw-jpS.PrivilegedSsh.sh ${BUZ_FOLIO} 'wsl.exe --import ${BUJB_wsl_distribution} C:\\WSL\\${BUJB_wsl_distribution} <rootfs.tar>'
"
}

# Step 6 -- workload-side round-trip validation (knock).
zbujb_garrison_step6_validate() {
  local z_letter="${1:-}"
  buc_step "  [6/6] Validate workload round-trip"

  local z_exit=0
  ssh -i "${BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE}"     \
      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                       \
      -o BatchMode=yes                            \
      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new         \
      -o ConnectTimeout=10                        \
      "${BURC_WORKLOAD_USER}@${BURN_HOST}" \
      true                                        \
    || z_exit=$?

  test "${z_exit}" -eq 0 \
    || buc_die "Workload round-trip failed (ssh exit ${z_exit}); the new account did not accept its own key."
}

######################################################################
# Public: Garrison ceremony

# bujb_garrison LETTER -- run the 6-step garrison ceremony for shell-letter
# b, c, or w. Caller must have invoked bujb_resolve_investiture beforehand.

content.text:
Got it — found the real bug. Here's the mechanism:

`zbujb_garrison_step1_admin_open` calls `zbujb_admin_exec w` (line 625) which wraps the remote command in `wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user root bash -s` (line 295). Since `rbtww-main` doesn't exist on bujn-winpc, wsl.exe fails — and step 1 attributes the non-zero exit to "Admin SSH failed" (exit 255), pointing at fenestrate.

The new pre-flight `zbujb_garrison_w_preflight` is wired on line 626, **after** step 1 — so it never gets to run and emit its proper absent-distro diagnostic with the `tt/buw-jpS` install hints.

The pre-flight itself is correctly written (uses bare ssh + powershell, no wsl.exe wrap). The bug is purely the wiring order.

**Repair (one-line swap at lines 625-626):**

```bash
test "${z_letter}" = "w" && zbujb_garrison_w_preflight
zbujb_garrison_step1_admin_open    "${z_letter}"
```

This matches the docket's wording precisely ("**prepend** a step ... that runs `wsl.exe --list --verbose` via admin SSH"). The implementation prepended the helper but appended the call.

Apply the fix?

1280:**The POSIX suppression rule**: `set -e` is suppressed inside `if`, `while`, `||`, `&&` test expressions, and this propagates through the **entire call tree** of the tested command.

1331:**BCG rule: Never `test ... && break/continue/return`.** Always invert the test and use `||`. The `||` form is already the BCG standard for control flow after a test; the `&&` form relies on humans remembering that bash exempts `&&`/`||` lists from `set -e`, which is a language-lawyer trap.
1333:### ❌ `A && B || C` as Pseudo-Ternary
1337:curl "${z_url}" && jq '.field' "${z_file}" || buc_die "Failed"
1347:**BCG rule: Never `A && B || C`.** This is not if-then-else. When `A` succeeds but `B` fails, `C` runs — which is almost never the intended behavior. Use explicit `if`/`then`/`else` for conditional execution with a fallback.

1310	|------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
1311	| Regular/enroll   | `\|\| buc_die`       | Enroll validates invariants; violations are fatal |
1312	| Predicate        | `\|\| return 1`      | Never dies, status only                          |
1313	| Capture/recite   | `\|\| return 1`      | Never dies, caller decides                       |
1314	| Yawp             | N/A                  | Cannot fail — pure builtins only                 |
1315	| Flow control     | `\|\| continue`      | Intentional skip to next iteration               |
1316	
1317	### ❌ Test-and-control-flow with `&&`
1318	
1319	```bash
1320	# ❌ Relies on set -e exemption for &&/|| lists — fragile
1321	test "${z_val}" = "done" && break
1322	test -z "${z_val}" && return 0
1323	[[ "${z_line}" == *"pattern"* ]] && continue
1324	
1325	# ✅ Invert the test, use || — same behavior, no exemption reliance
1326	test "${z_val}" != "done" || break
1327	test -n "${z_val}" || return 0
1328	[[ "${z_line}" != *"pattern"* ]] || continue
1329	```
1330	
1331	**BCG rule: Never `test ... && break/continue/return`.** Always invert the test and use `||`. The `||` form is already the BCG standard for control flow after a test; the `&&` form relies on humans remembering that bash exempts `&&`/`||` lists from `set -e`, which is a language-lawyer trap.
1332	
1333	### ❌ `A && B || C` as Pseudo-Ternary
1334	
1335	```bash
1336	# ❌ C runs both when A fails AND when B fails — not equivalent to if/then/else
1337	curl "${z_url}" && jq '.field' "${z_file}" || buc_die "Failed"
1338	
1339	# ✅ Explicit if/then/else — C runs only when A fails
1340	if curl "${z_url}"; then
1341	  jq '.field' "${z_file}" || buc_die "jq failed"
1342	else
1343	  buc_die "curl failed"
1344	fi
1345	```
1346	
1347	**BCG rule: Never `A && B || C`.** This is not if-then-else. When `A` succeeds but `B` fails, `C` runs — which is almost never the intended behavior. Use explicit `if`/`then`/`else` for conditional execution with a fallback.
1348	
1349	---
1350	
1351	## Array Safety Under `set -u`
1352	
1353	Under `set -u`, `"${array[@]}"` on an empty array triggers "unbound variable" in bash 3.2. Two patterns are safe; choose by whether you need the index.
1354	
1355	**Index iteration** — use when you need the index (parallel arrays, kindle rolls). Inherently safe on empty arrays, no guard needed:
1356	
1357	```bash
1358	for z_i in "${!z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
1359	  echo "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[$z_i]}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1360	done
1361	```
1362	
1363	**Guarded value iteration** — use when you only need values. The `(( ))` guard prevents expansion of the empty array:
1364	
1365	```bash
1366	if (( ${#z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]} )); then
1367	  for z_val in "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
1368	    echo "${z_val}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1369	  done

| Predicate        | `\|\| return 1`      | Never dies, status only                          |
| Capture/recite   | `\|\| return 1`      | Never dies, caller decides                       |
| Yawp             | N/A                  | Cannot fail — pure builtins only                 |
| Flow control     | `\|\| continue`      | Intentional skip to next iteration               |

### ❌ Test-and-control-flow with `&&`

```bash
# ❌ Relies on set -e exemption for &&/|| lists — fragile
test "${z_val}" = "done" && break
test -z "${z_val}" && return 0
[[ "${z_line}" == *"pattern"* ]] && continue

# ✅ Invert the test, use || — same behavior, no exemption reliance
test "${z_val}" != "done" || break
test -n "${z_val}" || return 0
[[ "${z_line}" != *"pattern"* ]] || continue
```

**BCG rule: Never `test ... && break/continue/return`.** Always invert the test and use `||`. The `||` form is already the BCG standard for control flow after a test; the `&&` form relies on humans remembering that bash exempts `&&`/`||` lists from `set -e`, which is a language-lawyer trap.

### ❌ `A && B || C` as Pseudo-Ternary

```bash
# ❌ C runs both when A fails AND when B fails — not equivalent to if/then/else
curl "${z_url}" && jq '.field' "${z_file}" || buc_die "Failed"

# ✅ Explicit if/then/else — C runs only when A fails
if curl "${z_url}"; then
  jq '.field' "${z_file}" || buc_die "jq failed"
else
  buc_die "curl failed"
fi
```

**BCG rule: Never `A && B || C`.** This is not if-then-else. When `A` succeeds but `B` fails, `C` runs — which is almost never the intended behavior. Use explicit `if`/`then`/`else` for conditional execution with a fallback.

---

## Array Safety Under `set -u`

Under `set -u`, `"${array[@]}"` on an empty array triggers "unbound variable" in bash 3.2. Two patterns are safe; choose by whether you need the index.

**Index iteration** — use when you need the index (parallel arrays, kindle rolls). Inherently safe on empty arrays, no guard needed:

```bash
for z_i in "${!z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
  echo "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[$z_i]}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
done
```

**Guarded value iteration** — use when you only need values. The `(( ))` guard prevents expansion of the empty array:

```bash
if (( ${#z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]} )); then
  for z_val in "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
    echo "${z_val}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
  done

1075	
1076	### ❌ Anti-Patterns
1077	
1078	```bash
1079	# Avoid [[ ]] for simple tests
1080	[[ -f "${z_file}" ]]           # Use: test -f "${z_file}"
1081	[[ "${z_var}" == "value" ]]    # Use: test "${z_var}" = "value"
1082	[[ -n "${z_param}" ]]          # Use: test -n "${z_param}"
1083	
1084	# Never use test with command substitution that might expand to empty
1085	# test with ZERO arguments returns TRUE (exit 0) — silent pass landmine
1086	test $(echo "${z_val}" | grep -E '^pattern$')  # ❌ Succeeds when grep matches nothing!
1087	
1088	# ✅ Use exit-status predicates instead
1089	echo "${z_val}" | grep -qE '^pattern$' || buc_die "Invalid format"
1090	
1091	# ✅ Or use case statement for pattern matching
1092	case "${z_val}" in
1093	  *pattern*) buc_log_args "Found pattern" ;;
1094	  *) buc_die "Pattern not found" ;;
1095	esac
1096	```
1097	
1098	**Note**: Use `=` not `==` in test expressions for POSIX compliance.
1099	
1100	### ❌ Subshell Exit vs Brace-Group
1101	
1102	```bash
1103	# ❌ exit in subshell kills only the subshell — script continues
1104	some_cmd || (echo "ERROR" >&2 && exit 1)
1105	
1106	# ✅ Brace-group stays in same process — exit/return works correctly
1107	some_cmd || { echo "ERROR" >&2; exit 1; }
1108	```
1109	
1110	**Rule**: Error blocks must use `{ ...; }` not `( ... )` when intending to exit or return. The `(subshell)` creates a new process — `exit` terminates only that subshell, not the calling script.
1111	
1112	### ✅ Legitimate Subshell Uses
1113	
1114	The prohibition above is specific to error-handling blocks where `exit`/`return` must reach the calling shell. Subshells are legitimate in these contexts:
1115	
1116	- **Test execution** — principled isolation between suites and cases; see **Test Execution Patterns**
1117	- **`$()` command substitution** — inside `_capture`/`_recite` functions only, or bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`); see **Capture Functions** and **Command Substitution Rules** checklist
1118	- **Isolation subshells** — environment containment with exit-status propagation (below)
1119	
1120	#### Isolation Subshells (environment containment)
1121	
1122	When a sequence of commands mutates the shell environment (sourcing files, `cd`, venv activation, `export`), a subshell prevents those mutations from leaking into subsequent commands. The exit status propagates across the `)` boundary, so the caller can detect and handle failures.
1123	
1124	**Rarely needed.** Most code operates within a single kindled environment. Use isolation subshells only when you must execute commands that change the environment and those changes must not persist.
1125	
1126	**Two variants** — same principle, different caller handling:
1127	
1128	```bash
1129	# Variant 1: Error propagation — die on any internal failure
1130	(
1131	  source "${z_config_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source: ${z_config_file}"
1132	  «prefix»_operation || buc_die "Operation failed"
1133	) || buc_die "Isolation subshell failed"
1134	
1135	# Variant 2: Status capture — caller inspects and decides
1136	local z_status=0
1137	(
1138	  source "${z_config_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source: ${z_config_file}"
1139	  «prefix»_operation || buc_die "Operation failed"
1140	) || z_status=$?
1141	```
1142	
1143	Inside the subshell, `buc_die` calls `exit` which terminates only the subshell — producing a non-zero exit status that the outer `|| buc_die` or `|| z_status=$?` catches. Failure propagates through exit status, not through `exit` reaching the parent.
1144	
1145	**Constraints:**
1146	- Every command inside must have explicit `|| buc_die` — `set -e` is suppressed inside `( ... ) ||` (as with all BCG code, never rely on it)
1147	- The outer boundary must have `|| buc_die` or `|| z_status=$?` — never leave the subshell exit status unchecked
1148	- Sourcing inside the subshell is permitted (this is a primary use case — isolation prevents sourced variables from leaking)
1149	- Keep subshell bodies short and focused — if the body grows complex, delegate to a CLI script via `exec`
1150	- `$( ... ) || buc_die` is legitimate when you need both isolation and output capture — `$()` is a subshell, so isolation holds and the two-line capture pattern applies
1151	
1152	**Use cases:**
1153	- Iterating config files that define overlapping variables (e.g., sourcing each nameplate `.env` in a loop)
1154	- Running commands that require `cd` to a different directory
1155	- Activating a virtual environment for a single operation
1156	- Any sequence where environment mutations must not outlive the block
1157	
1158	**Anti-pattern — no internal error handling:**
1159	
1160	```bash
1161	# ❌ Failures inside subshell are silent — source or echo can fail undetected
1162	(
1163	  source "${z_file}"
1164	  echo "${SOME_VAR}"
1165	) || buc_die "Subshell failed"
1166	
1167	# ✅ Every command explicitly handled
1168	(
1169	  source "${z_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source: ${z_file}"
1170	  echo "${SOME_VAR}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
1171	) || buc_die "Subshell failed"
1172	```
1173	
1174	---
1175	
1176	## Prohibited Constructs
1177	
1178	### ❌ HERE Documents (Heredocs)
1179	
1180	```bash
1181	# Never use - breaks indentation
1182	cat <<EOF > "${z_file}"
1183	content here

file.content:
### ❌ Anti-Patterns

```bash
# Avoid [[ ]] for simple tests
[[ -f "${z_file}" ]]           # Use: test -f "${z_file}"
[[ "${z_var}" == "value" ]]    # Use: test "${z_var}" = "value"
[[ -n "${z_param}" ]]          # Use: test -n "${z_param}"

# Never use test with command substitution that might expand to empty
# test with ZERO arguments returns TRUE (exit 0) — silent pass landmine
test $(echo "${z_val}" | grep -E '^pattern$')  # ❌ Succeeds when grep matches nothing!

# ✅ Use exit-status predicates instead
echo "${z_val}" | grep -qE '^pattern$' || buc_die "Invalid format"

# ✅ Or use case statement for pattern matching
case "${z_val}" in
  *pattern*) buc_log_args "Found pattern" ;;
  *) buc_die "Pattern not found" ;;
esac
```

**Note**: Use `=` not `==` in test expressions for POSIX compliance.

### ❌ Subshell Exit vs Brace-Group

```bash
# ❌ exit in subshell kills only the subshell — script continues
some_cmd || (echo "ERROR" >&2 && exit 1)

# ✅ Brace-group stays in same process — exit/return works correctly
some_cmd || { echo "ERROR" >&2; exit 1; }
```

**Rule**: Error blocks must use `{ ...; }` not `( ... )` when intending to exit or return. The `(subshell)` creates a new process — `exit` terminates only that subshell, not the calling script.

### ✅ Legitimate Subshell Uses

The prohibition above is specific to error-handling blocks where `exit`/`return` must reach the calling shell. Subshells are legitimate in these contexts:

- **Test execution** — principled isolation between suites and cases; see **Test Execution Patterns**
- **`$()` command substitution** — inside `_capture`/`_recite` functions only, or bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`); see **Capture Functions** and **Command Substitution Rules** checklist
- **Isolation subshells** — environment containment with exit-status propagation (below)

#### Isolation Subshells (environment containment)

When a sequence of commands mutates the shell environment (sourcing files, `cd`, venv activation, `export`), a subshell prevents those mutations from leaking into subsequent commands. The exit status propagates across the `)` boundary, so the caller can detect and handle failures.

**Rarely needed.** Most code operates within a single kindled environment. Use isolation subshells only when you must execute commands that change the environment and those changes must not persist.

**Two variants** — same principle, different caller handling:

```bash
# Variant 1: Error propagation — die on any internal failure
(
  source "${z_config_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source: ${z_config_file}"
  «prefix»_operation || buc_die "Operation failed"
) || buc_die "Isolation subshell failed"

# Variant 2: Status capture — caller inspects and decides
local z_status=0
(
  source "${z_config_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source: ${z_config_file}"
  «prefix»_operation || buc_die "Operation failed"
) || z_status=$?
```

Inside the subshell, `buc_die` calls `exit` which terminates only the subshell — producing a non-zero exit status that the outer `|| buc_die` or `|| z_status=$?` catches. Failure propagates through exit status, not through `exit` reaching the parent.

**Constraints:**
- Every command inside must have explicit `|| buc_die` — `set -e` is suppressed inside `( ... ) ||` (as with all BCG code, never rely on it)
- The outer boundary must have `|| buc_die` or `|| z_status=$?` — never leave the subshell exit status unchecked
- Sourcing inside the subshell is permitted (this is a primary use case — isolation prevents sourced variables from leaking)
- Keep subshell bodies short and focused — if the body grows complex, delegate to a CLI script via `exec`
- `$( ... ) || buc_die` is legitimate when you need both isolation and output capture — `$()` is a subshell, so isolation holds and the two-line capture pattern applies

**Use cases:**
- Iterating config files that define overlapping variables (e.g., sourcing each nameplate `.env` in a loop)
- Running commands that require `cd` to a different directory
- Activating a virtual environment for a single operation
- Any sequence where environment mutations must not outlive the block

**Anti-pattern — no internal error handling:**

```bash
# ❌ Failures inside subshell are silent — source or echo can fail undetected
(
  source "${z_file}"
  echo "${SOME_VAR}"
) || buc_die "Subshell failed"

# ✅ Every command explicitly handled
(
  source "${z_file}" || buc_die "Failed to source: ${z_file}"
  echo "${SOME_VAR}" || buc_die "Failed to echo"
) || buc_die "Subshell failed"
```

---

## Prohibited Constructs

### ❌ HERE Documents (Heredocs)

```bash
# Never use - breaks indentation
cat <<EOF > "${z_file}"
content here
must be unindented

1261	
1262	Every tolerance, alias, fallback, or alternative path multiplies the enumerated state space. The litmus test: **"How many zeroes got added to the enumerated state space because of this choice?"** If the answer isn't zero, the change requires explicit justification.
1263	
1264	### Rules
1265	
1266	1. **One canonical form**: Every command, argument, and parameter has exactly one accepted form. Do not accept alternatives.
1267	2. **No tolerances**: Do not add case folding, prefix stripping, alias acceptance, or alternative parse paths. If the canonical form is `--racing`, do not also accept `-r`, `racing`, or `RACING`.
1268	3. **No undocumented defaults**: Do not add `${1:-default}` patterns that silently fill missing required arguments. Required means required.
1269	4. **No silent normalization**: Do not silently transform input. If input doesn't match the canonical form, `buc_die` with a clear message stating the expected form.
1270	5. **Documentation uses canonical form**: All documentation, examples, and `buc_doc_*` output must show the single canonical invocation — never a tolerated alternative.
1271	
1272	### Smell Test
1273	
1274	If a command can be invoked two different ways for the same result, the interface is contaminated. If `"${1:-}"` silently provides a default for what should be a mandatory argument, the interface is contaminated.

1304

file.content:
### Zeroes Theory

Every tolerance, alias, fallback, or alternative path multiplies the enumerated state space. The litmus test: **"How many zeroes got added to the enumerated state space because of this choice?"** If the answer isn't zero, the change requires explicit justification.

### Rules

1. **One canonical form**: Every command, argument, and parameter has exactly one accepted form. Do not accept alternatives.
2. **No tolerances**: Do not add case folding, prefix stripping, alias acceptance, or alternative parse paths. If the canonical form is `--racing`, do not also accept `-r`, `racing`, or `RACING`.
3. **No undocumented defaults**: Do not add `${1:-default}` patterns that silently fill missing required arguments. Required means required.
4. **No silent normalization**: Do not silently transform input. If input doesn't match the canonical form, `buc_die` with a clear message stating the expected form.
5. **Documentation uses canonical form**: All documentation, examples, and `buc_doc_*` output must show the single canonical invocation — never a tolerated alternative.

### Smell Test

If a command can be invoked two different ways for the same result, the interface is contaminated. If `"${1:-}"` silently provides a default for what should be a mandatory argument, the interface is contaminated.

---

## `set -e` is Not Sufficient

**The POSIX suppression rule**: `set -e` is suppressed inside `if`, `while`, `||`, `&&` test expressions, and this propagates through the **entire call tree** of the tested command.

```bash
# ❌ set -e suppressed for entire call tree of some_function
if some_function; then ...

# Even if some_function calls other_function that fails,
# set -e will not terminate — suppression propagates
```

**BCG rule: Only `_predicate` functions may appear in `if`/`while` conditions.** All other functions must be invoked as simple commands with explicit `|| buc_die` / `|| return`. This completely prevents the suppression hazard.

```bash
# ✅ Predicate in conditional — designed for this, never dies, status only
if z«prefix»_ready_predicate; then ...

# ✅ Regular function — explicit error handling, not inside conditional
some_function || buc_die "..."

# ❌ Regular function in conditional — set -e suppressed for entire call tree
if some_function; then ...
```

**Why BCG's `|| buc_die` discipline works**: It doesn't rely on `set -e` — explicit error handling after every command means suppression doesn't matter. The `|| z_status=$?` capture pattern intentionally relies on this suppression behavior.

**Loops**: `set -e` does not reliably catch failures inside `for`/`while` loop bodies in bash 3.2. Every command in a loop body must have explicit error handling.

The error handling suffix depends on the function type:

| Function type    | On failure use       | Why                                             |
|------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| Regular/enroll   | `\|\| buc_die`       | Enroll validates invariants; violations are fatal |
| Predicate        | `\|\| return 1`      | Never dies, status only                          |
| Capture/recite   | `\|\| return 1`      | Never dies, caller decides                       |
| Yawp             | N/A                  | Cannot fail — pure builtins only                 |
| Flow control     | `\|\| continue`      | Intentional skip to next iteration               |

### ❌ Test-and-control-flow with `&&`

content.text:
Bash 3.2 chokes on `${var//$'\x00'/}` — the parser can't accept `$'\x00'` (NUL is bash's internal string terminator) inside parameter expansion. The `\r` strip on line 570 likely works but the NUL strip on 571 is the unparseable one.

`wsl.exe --list --quiet` emits UTF-16LE, so the NUL strip is essential to read the output as ASCII. Cleanest fix: do both byte strips via `tr` while reading the file in one operation.

input.new_string:
local z_output
  z_output=$(tr -d '\r\000' < "${ZBUJB_WSL_PREFLIGHT_STDOUT}") \
    || buc_die "Failed to read WSL preflight output: ${ZBUJB_WSL_PREFLIGHT_STDOUT}"

190:### Template 2: CLI Entry Point
502:All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.
516:# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
529:Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.
563:### String Usage
570:# Multi-line HERE-strings and HERE-docs prohibited
935:- Pure bash builtins only — no external commands, no process spawning
979:- Functions that call external commands → use `_capture` or temp files
1114:The prohibition above is specific to error-handling blocks where `exit`/`return` must reach the calling shell. Subshells are legitimate in these contexts:
1176:## Prohibited Constructs
1201:### ❌ Bare File Truncation
1317:### ❌ Test-and-control-flow with `&&`
1467:External commands are potential failure points, portability hazards, and supply-chain surface area. Every command invocation outside of bash builtins must earn its place.
1471:These external commands are accepted in any BUK-based project. Each has no bash 3.2 builtin replacement and is mandated by POSIX on any system that runs bash:
1509:### LLM Contributor Checkpoint
1611:### Module Structure
1664:- [ ] NO `$()` on external commands — use temp files; `_capture`/`_recite` for contracted functions only
1667:- [ ] Temp files for all external command output capture
1672:- [ ] `_yawp` functions use pure bash builtins only — no external commands
1713:### Sourcing Restrictions
1759:BCG prohibits subshells for error handling (see **Subshell Exit vs Brace-Group**). Test execution is the principled counterpoint: subshells provide **isolation boundaries** that prevent state leakage between fixtures and cases.
1868:### Suppressed Codes (BCG-structural)
1887:1. **No inline directives.** All structural suppressions live in `busc_shellcheckrc`. See **Prohibited Constructs § Inline Shellcheck Directives**.

content.content:
1465	## Command Dependency Discipline
1466	
1467	External commands are potential failure points, portability hazards, and supply-chain surface area. Every command invocation outside of bash builtins must earn its place.
1468	
1469	### POSIX Utility Allowlist
1470	
1471	These external commands are accepted in any BUK-based project. Each has no bash 3.2 builtin replacement and is mandated by POSIX on any system that runs bash:
1472	
1473	`chmod`, `cp`, `date`, `find`, `mkdir`, `mktemp`, `mv`, `rm`, `sed`, `sleep`, `sort`, `stty`
1474	
1475	Commands on this list need no justification — they are the irreducible dependency floor.
1476	
1477	### Evicted Utilities
1478	
1479	These commands have bash builtin or declared-dependency replacements. Do not reintroduce them.
1480	
1481	| Evicted | Replacement | Notes |
1482	|---------|-------------|-------|
1483	| `awk` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Field splitting is a builtin |
1484	| `base64` | `openssl enc -base64` | Platform flags differ (GNU `-d` vs BSD `-D`) |
1485	| `cut` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Same as awk — field extraction is a builtin |
1486	| `grep` | `case`, `test`, `[[ =~ ]]` | BCG-blessed pattern matching; see Test vs Bracket Expressions |
1487	| `head` | `read -r` | First-line extraction is a single builtin call |
1488	| `ls` | Glob expansion | `for f in dir/*` iterates without spawning a process |
1489	| `sha256sum`/`shasum` | `openssl dgst -sha256 -r` | Platform names differ; openssl is universal |
1490	| `tr` | `${var//old/new}` parameter expansion | Character replacement is a builtin |
1491	| `wc` | `${#var}`, `${#arr[@]}` | Length measurement is a builtin |
1492	
1493	### Declared Dependency Principle
1494	
1495	Anything beyond bash builtins and the POSIX allowlist is a **declared dependency** — a cost accepted by every consumer of the project. Declared dependencies must appear in the project's consumer-facing documentation (e.g., the dependency inventory in RBS0) with their justification.
1496	
1497	The current declared dependency for Recipe Bottle is `openssl`, which replaces three platform-variant commands (`base64`, `sha256sum`/`shasum`) with a single portable binary present on every target platform.
1498	
1499	### Platform-Variant Command Guidance
1500	
1501	Commands that exist on both GNU and BSD but with incompatible flags are the worst kind of dependency: they work on the developer's machine and fail on the consumer's. The canonical examples:
1502	
1503	- `base64 -d` (GNU) vs `base64 -D` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl enc -base64 -d`
1504	- `sha256sum` (GNU) vs `shasum -a 256` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl dgst -sha256 -r`
1505	- `stat -c` (GNU) vs `stat -f` (BSD) — wrap in a BUK function or avoid
1506	
1507	**Rule**: If a command's flags differ between GNU and BSD, either evict it in favor of a portable declared dependency or wrap it in a BUK function that normalizes the interface. Never rely on flag detection or platform sniffing at call sites.
1508	
1509	### LLM Contributor Checkpoint
1510	
1511	LLMs reach for whatever solves the immediate problem without feeling the cumulative dependency cost. A `grep -q` is two characters shorter than a `case` block and the model has seen it a million times — but it adds a process spawn, a portability surface, and an entry in the consumer's implicit dependency graph.
1512	
1513	The POSIX allowlist and eviction table serve as a checkpoint: if a command isn't on either list, stop and justify it before writing the code. The justification must answer: "Why can't a bash builtin or an already-declared dependency do this?"
1514	
1515	---
1516	
1517	## Output and Messaging Patterns
1518	
1519	### Message Hierarchy
1520	
1521	buc_step "Major user-visible operation"     # White, stderr, milestones
1522	buc_log_args "Detail from variables"        # Transcript only, from arguments
1523	command | buc_log_pipe                      # Transcript only, from pipeline
1524	buc_warn "Non-fatal warning"                # Yellow, stderr

file.content:
## Command Dependency Discipline

External commands are potential failure points, portability hazards, and supply-chain surface area. Every command invocation outside of bash builtins must earn its place.

### POSIX Utility Allowlist

These external commands are accepted in any BUK-based project. Each has no bash 3.2 builtin replacement and is mandated by POSIX on any system that runs bash:

`chmod`, `cp`, `date`, `find`, `mkdir`, `mktemp`, `mv`, `rm`, `sed`, `sleep`, `sort`, `stty`

Commands on this list need no justification — they are the irreducible dependency floor.

### Evicted Utilities

These commands have bash builtin or declared-dependency replacements. Do not reintroduce them.

| Evicted | Replacement | Notes |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| `awk` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Field splitting is a builtin |
| `base64` | `openssl enc -base64` | Platform flags differ (GNU `-d` vs BSD `-D`) |
| `cut` | `read` with IFS, parameter expansion | Same as awk — field extraction is a builtin |
| `grep` | `case`, `test`, `[[ =~ ]]` | BCG-blessed pattern matching; see Test vs Bracket Expressions |
| `head` | `read -r` | First-line extraction is a single builtin call |
| `ls` | Glob expansion | `for f in dir/*` iterates without spawning a process |
| `sha256sum`/`shasum` | `openssl dgst -sha256 -r` | Platform names differ; openssl is universal |
| `tr` | `${var//old/new}` parameter expansion | Character replacement is a builtin |
| `wc` | `${#var}`, `${#arr[@]}` | Length measurement is a builtin |

### Declared Dependency Principle

Anything beyond bash builtins and the POSIX allowlist is a **declared dependency** — a cost accepted by every consumer of the project. Declared dependencies must appear in the project's consumer-facing documentation (e.g., the dependency inventory in RBS0) with their justification.

The current declared dependency for Recipe Bottle is `openssl`, which replaces three platform-variant commands (`base64`, `sha256sum`/`shasum`) with a single portable binary present on every target platform.

### Platform-Variant Command Guidance

Commands that exist on both GNU and BSD but with incompatible flags are the worst kind of dependency: they work on the developer's machine and fail on the consumer's. The canonical examples:

- `base64 -d` (GNU) vs `base64 -D` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl enc -base64 -d`
- `sha256sum` (GNU) vs `shasum -a 256` (BSD) — evicted, use `openssl dgst -sha256 -r`
- `stat -c` (GNU) vs `stat -f` (BSD) — wrap in a BUK function or avoid

**Rule**: If a command's flags differ between GNU and BSD, either evict it in favor of a portable declared dependency or wrap it in a BUK function that normalizes the interface. Never rely on flag detection or platform sniffing at call sites.

### LLM Contributor Checkpoint

LLMs reach for whatever solves the immediate problem without feeling the cumulative dependency cost. A `grep -q` is two characters shorter than a `case` block and the model has seen it a million times — but it adds a process spawn, a portability surface, and an entry in the consumer's implicit dependency graph.

The POSIX allowlist and eviction table serve as a checkpoint: if a command isn't on either list, stop and justify it before writing the code. The justification must answer: "Why can't a bash builtin or an already-declared dependency do this?"

---

## Output and Messaging Patterns

### Message Hierarchy

buc_step "Major user-visible operation"     # White, stderr, milestones
buc_log_args "Detail from variables"        # Transcript only, from arguments
command | buc_log_pipe                      # Transcript only, from pipeline
buc_warn "Non-fatal warning"                # Yellow, stderr

content.text:
You're right — line 1490 evicts `tr` with replacement `${var//old/new}`. The architectural fix is to remove the NUL bytes at the source instead of stripping them in bash. PowerShell is decoding wsl.exe's UTF-16LE output but emitting it through a console encoding that preserves NUL interleaving.

The fix is server-side: set `[Console]::OutputEncoding` to UTF-8 in the PowerShell command. wsl.exe's output gets re-encoded as ASCII-clean bytes; bash reads cleanly with `$(<file)`; the `\r` strip alone (which works in 3.2) handles CRLF.

Let me revert the `tr` change and fix the PowerShell side instead.

zbujb_garrison_w_preflight() {
  zbujb_sentinel
  buc_step "  [w-preflight] Verify WSL distribution '${BUJB_wsl_distribution}' is installed"

  local z_exit=0
  ssh -i "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE}"            \
      -o IdentitiesOnly=yes                       \
      -o BatchMode=yes                            \
      -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new         \
      -o ConnectTimeout=15                        \
      "${BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER}@${BURN_HOST}"      \
      'powershell -NoProfile -Command "wsl.exe --list --quiet | Out-String"' \
      > "${ZBUJB_WSL_PREFLIGHT_STDOUT}"           \
      2> "${ZBUJB_WSL_PREFLIGHT_STDERR}"          \
    || z_exit=$?

  test "${z_exit}" -eq 0 \
    || buc_die "WSL list query failed (ssh exit ${z_exit}); see ${ZBUJB_WSL_PREFLIGHT_STDERR}"

  local z_output
  z_output=$(tr -d '\r\000' < "${ZBUJB_WSL_PREFLIGHT_STDOUT}") \
    || buc_die "Failed to read WSL preflight output: ${ZBUJB_WSL_PREFLIGHT_STDOUT}"

  case $'\n'"${z_output}"$'\n' in
    *$'\n'"${BUJB_wsl_distribution}"$'\n'*) return 0 ;;
  esac

  buc_die "WSL distribution '${BUJB_wsl_distribution}' (BUJB_wsl_distribution) not installed on ${BURN_HOST}.
